Example 119 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 119
Source Text
By using the term adaptation for the evolutionary process, and adaptive trait for the bodily part or function ( the product ), one may distinguish the two different senses of the word. Adaptation is one of the two main processes that explain the observed diversity of species, such as the different species of Darwin 's finches.
Generated Simplification
Adaptation is a word with two meanings in science. - **Adaptation as a process**: This means how living things change over time to survive better in their environment. - **Adaptive trait**: This means a body part or function that helps a living thing survive. For example, scientists study how animals like Darwin's finches have different traits. These traits help them live in different places. This shows how adaptation works.
Reference Simplification
By using the term adaptation for the evolutionary process, and adaptive trait for the bodily part or function ( the product ), the two senses of the word may be distinguished.

0.244

Example 121 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 121
Source Text
Genitourinary syndrome of menopause includes symptoms and signs due to estrogen deficiency such as Vulvovaginal atrophy; Urinary urgency; Dysuria; Frequent UTIs.
Generated Simplification
Genitourinary syndrome of menopause happens because the body has less estrogen. This can cause different problems. Here are some common symptoms: - The vaginal area may feel dry or irritated. This is called vulvovaginal atrophy. - You might feel a sudden need to urinate, called urinary urgency. - It can hurt or burn when you urinate. This is called dysuria. - You may get urinary tract infections (UTIs) more often. If you have these symptoms, talk to your doctor. They can help you find the right treatment.
Reference Simplification
Genitourinary syndrome of menopause is a new, more accurate term used to refer to symptoms that affect the vagina and urinary tract and that are caused by menopause, such as vaginal dryness, pain during sexual intercourse, urinary urgency, and urinary tract infections.

0.439

Example 123 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 123
Source Text
However, compared with Alzheimer disease, vascular dementia tends to cause memory loss later and to affect executive function earlier.
Generated Simplification
Vascular dementia and Alzheimer’s disease are different in how they affect the brain. - In vascular dementia, problems with memory usually happen later. - In Alzheimer’s disease, memory loss often happens earlier. - Vascular dementia can cause problems with planning, decision-making, and organizing (called executive function) earlier than Alzheimer’s disease. If you have questions about these conditions, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
However, compared with Alzheimer disease, vascular dementia tends to cause memory loss later and to affect judgment and personality less. Vascular dementia tends to cause difficulty planning and initiating actions earlier than Alzheimer disease.

Example 137 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 137
Source Text
Filamentation is the anomalous growth of certain bacteria, such as E. coli, in which cells continue to elongate but do not divide ( no septa formation ).
Generated Simplification
Filamentation happens when some bacteria, like E. coli, grow in an unusual way. - The bacteria cells become longer and longer. - However, they do not split into two new cells as they normally would. - This means the bacteria do not form the dividing walls (called septa) that are needed for splitting.
Reference Simplification
Filamentation is the anomalous ( not normal ) growth of certain bacteria, such as E. coli, in which cells continue to elongate but do not divide ( no septa formation ).

Example 140 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 140
Source Text
Thalassemia can cause significant complications, including iron overload, bone deformities and cardiovascular illness.
Generated Simplification
Thalassemia is a blood condition that can lead to health problems. Here are some possible issues it can cause: - Too much iron in the body (iron overload), which can harm organs. - Changes in the shape of bones (bone deformities). - Problems with the heart and blood vessels (cardiovascular illness). If you have thalassemia, talk to your doctor about how to manage these risks.
Reference Simplification
Thalassemia can cause significant complications, including pneumonia, iron overload, bone deformities and cardiovascular illness.

Example 141 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 141
Source Text
Lyme disease has 3 stages: Early localized; Early disseminated; Late.
Generated Simplification
Lyme disease happens in three stages: 1. **Early Localized Stage** This is the first stage. Symptoms usually appear soon after a tick bite. 2. **Early Disseminated Stage** This is the second stage. The infection starts to spread to other parts of the body. 3. **Late Stage** This is the third stage. It happens if the disease is not treated earlier. If you think you might have Lyme disease, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
Lyme disease has three stages: Early localized; Early disseminated (widespread); Late.

Example 142 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 142
Source Text
The nervous system consists of a ventral nerve cord, with one ganglion in each segment, and an anterior nerve ring surrounding the pharynx.
Generated Simplification
The nervous system is an important part of the body. It helps control how the body works. - It has a main part called the ventral nerve cord. This is like a long cord running through the body. - There is one small group of nerve cells, called a ganglion, in each body segment. These ganglia help control different parts of the body. - At the front of the body, near the throat (pharynx), there is a nerve ring. This ring surrounds the pharynx and helps connect the nervous system.
Reference Simplification
It consists of a ventral nerve cord, with one ganglion in each segment, and an anterior nerve ring surrounding the pharynx.

Example 144 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 144
Source Text
MRI coupled with magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) is now frequently used for diagnosis and can show masses in the pancreas as well as provide more optimal visualization of ductal changes consistent with chronic pancreatitis.
Generated Simplification
An MRI is a type of scan that takes detailed pictures of the inside of your body. Sometimes, an MRI is combined with another special scan called MRCP. MRCP stands for magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography. This scan looks closely at the pancreas and the ducts (small tubes) connected to it. Doctors use these scans to: - Find any lumps or masses in the pancreas. - Check for changes in the ducts that might happen with a condition called chronic pancreatitis. If you have questions about these scans, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
MRCP shows the bile and pancreatic ducts more clearly than does CT.

When she was a child, she had several health problems: - Her lungs partially collapsed two times. - She had pneumonia four to five times every year. These were serious conditions that needed medical care.

Example 156 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 156
Source Text
Complicated UTIs are more difficult to treat and usually requires more aggressive evaluation, treatment and follow-up.
Generated Simplification
Some urinary tract infections (UTIs) are harder to treat. These are called complicated UTIs. - Treating a complicated UTI may take more time and stronger medicines. - Doctors may need to do extra tests to check what is causing the infection. - After treatment, follow-up care is often needed to make sure the infection is gone. If you think you have a UTI, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
A complicated UTI is more difficult to treat and usually needs more aggressive evaluation, treatment, and follow-up.

Example 162 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 162
Source Text
Nonconvulsive status epilepticus is being increasingly recognized as a cause of altered mental status in ICU patients.
Generated Simplification
Nonconvulsive status epilepticus (NCSE) is a condition that can affect the brain. - It can cause changes in how a person thinks or acts. - This condition is being found more often in patients in the intensive care unit (ICU). If you or a loved one is in the ICU and shows unusual behavior or confusion, talk to the healthcare team. They can check for NCSE and provide the right care.
Reference Simplification
People in ICUs may have seizures that do not cause convulsions (called nonconvulsive seizures). These seizures can cause delirium, but the seizures may not be recognized because they do not cause convulsions or other typical symptoms of seizures. If the seizures are not recognized, they may not be treated appropriately and promptly.

Source Text
Gout can present in a number of ways, although the most usual is a recurrent attack of acute inflammatory arthritis ( a red, tender, hot, swollen joint ).
Generated Simplification
Gout can show up in different ways. The most common sign of gout is a sudden attack of joint pain. This is called acute inflammatory arthritis. During an attack: - The joint may become red. - It can feel tender to the touch. - The area might feel hot. - The joint may swell up. If you think you have these symptoms, talk to a doctor.

Gout can present in a number of ways, although the most common is a recurrent attack of intense inflammatory arthritis ( a red, tender, hot, swollen joint ).

Example 167 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 167
Source Text
Olfactory seizures, manifested as an aura of foul smells (rotten eggs, burnt meat), indicate temporal lobe involvement and suggest HSV encephalitis.
Generated Simplification
Some seizures can cause a person to smell bad odors that are not really there. These smells might seem like rotten eggs or burnt meat. This type of seizure is called an "olfactory seizure." It often happens because of a problem in a part of the brain called the temporal lobe. In some cases, this can be a sign of a serious brain infection called HSV encephalitis. If you or someone you know experiences this, it is important to talk to a doctor right away.
Reference Simplification
People also have seizures, sometimes accompanied by strange smells (such as rotten eggs), vivid flashbacks, or sudden, intense emotions.

Example 171 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 171
Source Text
Pain is usually felt over the inflamed site, but referred pain is possible.

Pain often happens in the area that is inflamed or hurt. Sometimes, pain can be felt in a different part of the body. This is called "referred pain."
Reference Simplification
The pain is usually felt in the chest wall right over the site of the inflammation or infection that caused the effusion.

Example 172 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 172
Source Text
Pyelonephritis is treated more aggressively than a simple bladder infection using either a longer course of oral antibiotics or intravenous antibiotics.
Generated Simplification
Pyelonephritis is a kidney infection. It needs stronger treatment than a bladder infection. Treatment options include: - Taking antibiotics by mouth for a longer time. - Getting antibiotics through a needle into a vein (this is called intravenous antibiotics). Your doctor will decide the best treatment for you.
Reference Simplification
Pyelonephritis ( kidney infection ) is treated more aggressively than a simple bladder infection using either a longer course of oral antibiotics or intravenous antibiotics.

Example 174 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 174
Source Text
Patients are taller than average for age and family; arm span exceeds height. Arachnodactyly (disproportionately long, thin digits) is noticeable, often by the thumb sign (the distal phalanx of the thumb protrudes beyond the edge of the clenched fist).
Generated Simplification
Some people with this condition are taller than usual for their age or family. Their arm span (the distance from fingertip to fingertip when arms are stretched out) is often longer than their height. They may also have long, thin fingers. This is called arachnodactyly. A common sign of this is the "thumb sign." In this case, when the person makes a fist, the tip of their thumb sticks out past the edge of the fist.
Reference Simplification
People with Marfan syndrome are taller than expected for their age and family. Their arm span (the distance between fingertips when the arms are outstretched) is greater than their height. Their fingers are long and thin.

Example 175 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 175
Source Text
Patients may present with systemic symptoms such as fever (usually low - grade), fatigue, malaise, unexplained weight loss, and sweats. Some patients are initially diagnosed as having fever of unknown origin.
Generated Simplification
You may experience symptoms that affect your whole body. These can include: - A mild fever (your body feels warmer than usual). - Feeling very tired or weak. - A general feeling of being unwell. - Losing weight without trying. - Sweating a lot, especially at night. Sometimes, doctors may not know the exact cause of your fever at first. This is called a "fever of unknown origin." If you have these symptoms, talk to your doctor so they can find out what is causing them.
Reference Simplification
People may have fever and feel tired and generally unwell. They may lose weight unintentionally and sweat more than usual.

Example 176 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 176
Source Text
Patients suspected of having stomach cancer should have endoscopy with multiple biopsies and brush cytology. Occasionally, a biopsy limited to the mucosa misses tumor tissue in the submucosa.
Generated Simplification
If your doctor thinks you might have stomach cancer, they will recommend a test called an endoscopy. - During an endoscopy, a thin tube with a camera is used to look inside your stomach. - The doctor will take small tissue samples, called biopsies, from different areas of your stomach. - They may also collect cells using a gentle brushing technique, called brush cytology. Sometimes, a biopsy might not find cancer if it is deeper in the stomach wall. This is because the biopsy only takes tissue from the surface layer (called the mucosa). If this happens, more tests may be needed. Talk to your doctor if you have questions about these tests.
Reference Simplification
Endoscopy (an examination in which a flexible tube is used to visualize the inside of the digestive tract) is the best diagnostic procedure. It allows a doctor to view the stomach directly, to check for Helicobacter pylori, and to remove tissue samples for examination under a microscope (biopsy).
BERTScore
Precision
0.056
Recall
0.358
F1
0.204

Example 177 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 177
Source Text
Rates of asymptomatic bacteria in the urine increase with age from two to seven percent in women of child bearing age to as high as 50 % in elderly women in care homes.
Generated Simplification
As women get older, it becomes more common to have bacteria in their urine without any symptoms. - In women who can have children, this happens in about 2 to 7 out of 100 women. - In elderly women living in care homes, it can happen in up to 50 out of 100 women. This means the chance of having bacteria in the urine increases with age.
Reference Simplification
Rates of asymptomatic bacteria in the urine increase with age from 2 % to 7 % in women of childbearing age to as high as 50 % in elderly women in care homes.

Example 179 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 179
Source Text
Seven days of the oral fluoroquinolone ciprofloxacin is typically used in areas where the resistance rate is less than 10 %.
Generated Simplification
Ciprofloxacin is a type of antibiotic. It is often used to treat infections. In some areas, ciprofloxacin works well for most people. This is when less than 10% of bacteria in that area are resistant to it. If ciprofloxacin is used, it is usually taken by mouth for 7 days. Talk to your doctor to find out if ciprofloxacin is the right treatment for you.
Reference Simplification
Seven days of the oral fluoroquinolone ciprofloxacin is typically used in geographic areas where the resistance rate is less than 10 %.

Example 180 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 180
Source Text
The study also indicated that increased ejaculation through masturbation rather than intercourse would be more helpful as intercourse is associated with diseases ( STDs ) that may increase the risk of cancer instead.
Generated Simplification
The study found that ejaculating more often may help reduce the risk of certain cancers. Masturbation is a safer way to do this compared to sexual intercourse. This is because sexual intercourse can sometimes lead to sexually transmitted diseases (STDs). STDs may increase the risk of cancer. If you have questions about this, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
The study also indicated that this would be more helpful than ejaculation through sexual intercourse because intercourse can transmit diseases that may increase the risk of cancer instead.

Example 181 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 181
Source Text
Waist circumference and BP
Generated Simplification
### Waist Circumference and Blood Pressure (BP) #### Waist Circumference - Waist circumference is the measurement around your waist. - It helps to check if you have too much fat around your belly. - Too much belly fat can increase the risk of health problems like heart disease and diabetes. #### Blood Pressure (BP) - Blood pressure is the force of your blood pushing against the walls of your blood vessels. - High blood pressure (also called hypertension) can harm your heart, kidneys, and other organs. - Low blood pressure can also cause problems, like feeling dizzy or fainting. #### Why These Are Important Together - A larger waist size can sometimes lead to higher blood pressure. - Keeping a healthy waist size and normal blood pressure can lower your risk of serious health problems. #### What You Can Do - Talk to your doctor about checking your waist size and blood pressure. - Your doctor can help you understand your results and what steps to take if needed.
Reference Simplification
Waist circumference Blood pressure measurement

Example 185 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 185
Source Text
A peripheral ring scotoma (detectable by visual field testing) widens gradually, and central vision may also be affected in advanced cases.
Generated Simplification
A peripheral ring scotoma is a problem with your vision. - It means there is a ring-shaped area around the edges of your vision where you cannot see clearly. - This can be found during a special eye test called a visual field test. Over time, this problem can get worse. - The blurry or missing area may become larger. - In more serious cases, it can also affect the center of your vision. If you notice changes in your vision, talk to your eye doctor.
Reference Simplification
In the late stages of the disease, the person typically has only a small area of central vision and possibly no peripheral vision remaining (tunnel vision). Sometimes in late stages the macula, the area of the retina responsible for central vision, swells. This swelling is called macular edema and affects central vision.

0.419

Example 186 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 186
Source Text
for kids Bruises in sports
Generated Simplification
### Bruises in Kids Who Play Sports When kids play sports, they might get bruises. A bruise happens when small blood vessels under the skin break. This can cause the skin to look purple, blue, or black. #### What Causes Bruises? - Bruises can happen if a child falls, bumps into something, or gets hit during sports. - Contact sports like football, soccer, or basketball can lead to more bruises because players often collide with each other. #### What Do Bruises Look Like? - At first, the bruise may look red or purple. - Over time, it might turn green, yellow, or brown as it heals. - Bruises can feel sore or tender when touched. #### How to Help a Bruise Heal 1. **Rest the area**: Try not to use the injured part too much. 2. **Ice the bruise**: Put a cold pack or ice wrapped in a cloth on the bruise for 15–20 minutes. Do this a few times a day for the first 1–2 days. 3. **Elevate the area**: If the bruise is on the leg or arm, keep it raised to reduce swelling. #### When to See a Doctor - If the bruise is very large or painful. - If the bruise doesn’t get better after a week. - If your child gets bruises often without any clear reason. - If the bruise is near the eye and causes vision problems. #### How to Prevent Bruises - Make sure your child wears the right safety gear, like helmets, pads, or shin guards. - Teach your child to play safely and follow the rules of the sport. If you are worried about a bruise or have questions, talk to your child’s doctor.
Reference Simplification
for kids Bruises in sports Fake bruises as fashion

Example 187 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 187
Source Text
Monozygotic twins have a concordance of about 45 %.
Generated Simplification
Identical twins (also called monozygotic twins) share the same genes because they come from the same fertilized egg. If one identical twin has a certain condition, the other twin has about a 45% chance of having the same condition. This means they do not always both have the condition, even though they share the same genes.
Reference Simplification
An identical twin whose co-twin has schizophrenia has about a 50 % risk of developing schizophrenia.

Example 189 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 189
Source Text
Advanced disease can lead to destruction of supporting cartilage, causing floppy ears, saddle nose, pectus excavatum, and visual, auditory, and vestibular abnormalities.
Generated Simplification
When a disease becomes severe, it can damage the cartilage in the body. Cartilage is a flexible tissue that supports different parts of the body. Here are some problems that can happen if cartilage is damaged: - **Floppy ears**: The ears may lose their shape and become soft. - **Saddle nose**: The bridge of the nose may collapse, making the nose look flat or sunken. - **Pectus excavatum**: This means the chest may sink inward. - **Vision problems**: The eyes may not work as well, causing trouble seeing. - **Hearing problems**: The ears may not work properly, leading to difficulty hearing. - **Balance problems**: The part of the body that helps with balance may not work well, making it harder to stay steady. If you notice any of these changes, talk to your doctor. They can help check what is causing the problem and suggest treatment.
Reference Simplification
Eventually, the supporting cartilage can be damaged, resulting in floppy ears, a sloping saddle nose, and a hollow at the lower part of the chest (pectus excavatum). The nerve in the inner ear can be affected, causing eventual problems with balance and hearing, and eventually vision problems can occur.

0.339

Example 191 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 191
Source Text
Nephritis is inflammation of the kidneys and may involve the glomeruli, tubules, or interstitial tissue surrounding the glomeruli and tubules.
Generated Simplification
Nephritis means that your kidneys are swollen or inflamed. This swelling can happen in different parts of the kidneys: - The glomeruli, which are tiny filters in the kidneys. - The tubules, which are small tubes that help clean your blood. - The tissue around these filters and tubes. If you think you might have a problem with your kidneys, talk to a doctor.
Reference Simplification
It may involve the glomeruli, tubules, or interstitial tissue surrounding the glomeruli and tubules. Nephritis is often caused by infections, and toxins, but is most commonly caused by autoimmune disorders that affect the major organs like kidneys. Nephritis can produce glomerular injury, by disturbing the glomerular structure with inflammatory cell proliferation.

Example 193 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 193
Source Text
Nasogastric suction
Generated Simplification
**Nasogastric Suction** Nasogastric suction is a medical procedure. It uses a thin, flexible tube called a nasogastric (NG) tube. This tube goes through your nose, down your throat, and into your stomach. ### Why is it done? Nasogastric suction is used to: - Remove fluid, air, or harmful substances from your stomach. - Help your stomach rest after surgery or an injury. - Treat blockages in your intestines. - Collect stomach contents for testing. ### How does it work? - The NG tube is gently inserted through your nose. - It is passed down your throat and into your stomach. - A machine or syringe is used to suction out stomach contents through the tube. ### What to expect during the procedure: - You may feel some discomfort when the tube is inserted. - You will be asked to swallow or sip water to help the tube go down. - The healthcare team will make sure the tube is in the right place before starting suction. ### Things to know: - The procedure is usually safe, but it may cause a sore throat, nose irritation, or mild bleeding. - Rarely, the tube may go into the wrong place, like your lungs. The healthcare team will check to avoid this. - If you feel pain or trouble breathing during the procedure, tell your healthcare provider right away. ### After the procedure: - The tube may stay in place for a short time or longer, depending on your condition. - Your healthcare provider will explain how long the tube will be needed. If you have any questions or concerns, talk to your healthcare provider.
Reference Simplification
Suction via nasogastric tube.

Precision
-0.449
Recall
0.381
F1
-0.068

Example 194 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 194
Source Text
Symptoms and signs of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis typically develop over 6 mo to several years and include dyspnea on exertion and nonproductive cough.
Generated Simplification
Symptoms of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) usually appear slowly over time. Here are the common symptoms: - Feeling short of breath, especially when moving or exercising. - A dry cough that does not bring up mucus. These symptoms can take 6 months to several years to develop. If you notice these signs, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
Symptoms depend on the extent of the lung damage, the rate at which the disease progresses, and whether complications, such as lung infections and right-sided heart failure (cor pulmonale) develop. The main symptoms start insidiously as shortness of breath during exertion, cough, and diminished stamina. In most people, symptoms worsen over a period ranging from about 6 months to several years.

Example 197 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 197
Source Text
Symptoms include rectal itching, a cloudy rectal discharge, bleeding, and constipation âall of varying severity. Examination with a proctoscope may detect erythema or mucopurulent exudate on the rectal wall.
Generated Simplification
Some symptoms you might notice include: - Itching around the rectum (the area at the end of your bowel). - A cloudy fluid coming from the rectum. - Bleeding from the rectum. - Trouble passing stools (constipation). These symptoms can be mild or more severe. Doctors may use a tool called a proctoscope to look inside the rectum. This tool helps them check for: - Redness or irritation inside the rectum. - A thick, mucus-like fluid on the rectal wall. If you have these symptoms, talk to your doctor. They can check what’s causing the problem and suggest the right treatment.
Reference Simplification
This infection usually causes no symptoms, but it may make bowel movements painful. Other symptoms include constipation, itching, bleeding, and a discharge from the rectum. The area around the anus may become red and raw, and stool may be coated with mucus and pus. When a doctor examines the rectum with a viewing tube (anoscope), mucus and pus may be visible on the wall of the rectum.

Example 198 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 198
Source Text
Ascites, splenomegaly, or portosystemic encephalopathy may be present.
Generated Simplification
You may have one or more of the following conditions: - **Ascites**: This means there is extra fluid in your belly. It can make your stomach look or feel swollen. - **Splenomegaly**: This means your spleen is larger than normal. The spleen is an organ in your body that helps fight infections and filters your blood. - **Portosystemic encephalopathy**: This is a condition that can affect your brain. It happens when toxins build up in your body because your liver is not working well. It can cause confusion, trouble thinking, or feeling very sleepy. If you think you have any of these problems, talk to your doctor. They can check your symptoms and help you.
Reference Simplification
If a large amount of fluid accumulates in the abdomen, the abdomen swells (distends), sometimes noticeably and sometimes enough to make the abdomen greatly enlarged and taut. This distention can be uncomfortable or painful. An enlarged spleen may cause a vague sense of discomfort in the upper left part of the abdomen.

Example 200 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 200
Source Text
Obstetric ultrasonography should be done to rule out hydatidiform mole and multifetal pregnancy.
Generated Simplification
An ultrasound scan is important during pregnancy. - This scan helps check for certain conditions. - It can rule out (make sure you don’t have) a hydatidiform mole. This is a rare problem where abnormal tissue grows in the womb instead of a baby. - The scan can also check if you are carrying more than one baby (a multifetal pregnancy). Talk to your doctor about getting this scan.
Reference Simplification
Ultrasonography is usually done to determine whether women have a hydatidiform mole (a disorder that initially resembles pregnancy) or more than one fetus (multiple births).

If you have questions about IV fluids, talk to your doctor or nurse.

Example 202 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 202
Source Text
The peripheral nervous system ( PNS ) is the part of the nervous system consisting of the nerves and ganglia outside of the brain and spinal cord.
Generated Simplification
The peripheral nervous system, or PNS, is part of your body's nervous system. - It includes the nerves and small groups of nerve cells (called ganglia). - These are found outside your brain and spinal cord. The PNS helps send messages between your brain, spinal cord, and the rest of your body.

The Peripheral nervous system, or PNS, is part of the nervous system, and consists of the nerves and neurons that are outside the central nervous system ( the brain and spinal cord ) and serve the limbs and organs, for example.

Example 203 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 203
Source Text
Bronchiectasis is best considered the common end-point of various disorders that cause chronic airway inflammation.
Generated Simplification
Bronchiectasis is a lung condition. It happens when different illnesses or problems cause long-term swelling (inflammation) in the airways. This swelling can damage the airways over time.
Reference Simplification
The most common cause is severe or repeated respiratory infections, which are particularly likely in people who have one or more of the following: Cystic fibrosis; Other hereditary disorders, such as primary ciliary dyskinesia, in which the ability to clear the airways is impaired; Immunodeficiency disorders; Certain autoimmune disorders, such as rheumatoid arthritis, ulcerative colitis, or Sjogren syndrome; Mechanical factors, such as airway blockage caused by an inhaled object, chronically enlarged lymph nodes, changes after lung surgery, or a lung tumor; Inhaling toxic substances that injure the airways, such as noxious fumes, gases, smoke (including tobacco smoke), and injurious dust (for example, silica and coal dust).

A water crisis is a situation where the available supply of potable, clean water within an area is less than the demand for it. Example 207 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 207
Source Text
Pain occurs along the distribution of one or more sensory divisions of the trigeminal nerve, most often the maxillary.
Generated Simplification
Pain can happen in areas connected to the trigeminal nerve. The trigeminal nerve is a large nerve in your face. It has three main branches: - The upper branch affects areas like your forehead and eyes. - The middle branch (called the maxillary branch) affects areas like your cheeks, upper jaw, and upper teeth. - The lower branch affects areas like your lower jaw and lower teeth. Pain is most common in the middle branch (maxillary branch).
Reference Simplification
Pain due to trigeminal neuralgia can occur spontaneously but is often triggered by touching a particular spot (called a trigger point) on the face, lips, or tongue or by an action such as brushing the teeth or chewing.

Source Text
A corneal ulcer begins as a corneal epithelial defect that stains with fluorescein and an underlying dull, grayish, circumscribed superficial opacity (representing an infiltrate).
Generated Simplification
A corneal ulcer is a problem with the clear front part of your eye, called the cornea. - It starts with damage to the outer layer of the cornea. - This damage can be seen using a special dye called fluorescein. - The ulcer may look like a dull, grayish spot on the cornea. This spot is caused by a group of immune cells called an infiltrate. If you think you might have a corneal ulcer, talk to your eye doctor right away.
Reference Simplification
The ulcer often appears as a white or dull and grayish spot on the cornea.

Example 212 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 212
Source Text
From May to October 2000, an outbreak of tularemia in Martha 's Vineyard resulted in one fatality, and brought the interest of the CDC as a potential investigative ground for aerosolized Francisella tularensis.

In 2000, there was an outbreak of a disease called tularemia on Martha's Vineyard. This happened between May and October. Tularemia is caused by a germ called *Francisella tularensis*. It can spread in different ways, including through the air. During this outbreak, one person died. Because of this, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) became involved. They wanted to study how the disease might spread through the air. If you have questions about tularemia or think you might be at risk, talk to your doctor.

It brought the interest of the CDC as a potential investigative ground for aerosolized Francisella tularensis.

Example 214 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 214

Women may not bond with their infant, resulting in emotional, social, and cognitive problems in the child later.

Some women may have trouble feeling close to their baby after birth. This can affect the baby’s emotions, social skills, and learning as they grow. If you feel this way, it’s important to talk to a healthcare professional. They can help you and your baby.

As a result, the child may have emotional, social, and cognitive problems later.

Example 215 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 215
Source Text
90.9 % of these were classified as having been done by `` curettage '' (suction-aspiration, dilation and curettage, dilation and evacuation), 7.7 % by `` medical '' means (mifepristone), 0.4 % by `` intrauterine instillation '' (saline or prostaglandin), and 1.0 % by `` other '' (including hysterotomy and hysterectomy).
Generated Simplification
Most of these procedures (90.9%) were done using a method called "curettage." This includes techniques like suction-aspiration, dilation and curettage (D&C), or dilation and evacuation (D&E). A smaller number (7.7%) were done using medicine, such as mifepristone. Very few (0.4%) were done by putting a liquid, like saline or prostaglandin, into the uterus. This is called "intrauterine instillation." About 1% were done using other methods, such as surgery (hysterotomy or hysterectomy).
Reference Simplification
90.9 % were done by curettage, 7.7 % were medical abortions (using drugs, mifepristone in most cases), 0.4 % by `` intrauterine instillation '' (saline or prostaglandin), and 1.0 % by `` other '' (including hysterotomy and hysterectomy).

Example 217 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 217
Source Text
Anteroposterior, lateral, and usually oblique x-rays are taken. A fracture, if present, is usually visible on the lateral view. X-rays may be normal if only the tendon is torn.
Generated Simplification
Doctors usually take three types of X-ray pictures: front view (anteroposterior), side view (lateral), and sometimes an angled view (oblique). If a bone is broken (fracture), it is usually seen in the side view X-ray. If only a tendon (the tissue that connects muscle to bone) is torn, the X-rays might look normal.
Reference Simplification
X-rays are usually taken at several different angles to check for a fracture.

Example 220 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 220
Source Text
Because the heterozygote survives , so does the HgbS allele survive at a rate much higher than the mutation rate ( see and refs in Sickle-cell disease ) .
Generated Simplification
The HgbS allele is a type of gene change linked to sickle cell disease. People who have one copy of this gene (called heterozygotes) can survive better in certain conditions. Because of this, the HgbS allele is passed down more often than it would be if it only appeared by chance or mutation. If you have questions about this, talk to your doctor.

Example 222 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 222
Source Text
Crohn disease should be suspected in a patient with inflammatory or obstructive symptoms or in a patient without prominent GI symptoms but with perianal fistulas or abscesses or with otherwise unexplained arthritis, erythema nodosum, fever, anemia, or (in a child) stunted growth. A family history of Crohn disease also increases the index of suspicion.
Generated Simplification
Crohn's disease is a condition that affects the digestive system. It can cause different symptoms. Here are some signs that may suggest Crohn's disease: - **Digestive problems**: These include inflammation (swelling) or blockage in the digestive system. - **Perianal issues**: This means problems around the anus, such as fistulas (abnormal connections between tissues) or abscesses (painful, swollen areas filled with pus). - **Other symptoms**: Some people may not have obvious digestive problems but might experience: - Unexplained joint pain or swelling (arthritis). - Red, painful lumps under the skin (erythema nodosum). - Fever without a clear cause. - Low red blood cell levels (anemia). - Poor growth in children. If someone in your family has Crohn's disease, your chances of having it may be higher. If you notice these symptoms, talk to a doctor. They can help figure out if Crohn's disease is the cause.
Reference Simplification
A doctor may suspect Crohn disease in a person with recurring crampy abdominal pain and diarrhea, particularly if the person has a family history of Crohn disease or a history of problems around the anus.
BERTScore
Precision
-0.073
Recall
0.293
F1
0.105

Example 224 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 224
Source Text
However, many also go on to form abscesses, which require surgical drainage; an extensive procedure is done with accompanying IV antibiotics.
Generated Simplification
Sometimes, infections can lead to the formation of abscesses. An abscess is a pocket of pus that forms in the body due to infection. If this happens, it may need to be treated with surgery. The surgery involves draining the pus from the abscess. After the procedure, you may also need medicine called antibiotics. These are often given through a tube into your vein (this is called IV antibiotics). Talk to your doctor if you think you might have an abscess or need more information about this treatment.
Reference Simplification
Abscesses must be drained surgically.

The inner ear is a part of the body that helps with hearing and balance. - It is located inside the skull, in a bone called the temporal bone. - Inside this area, there is a hollow space with special pathways. - These pathways are part of a system called the bony labyrinth. All animals with backbones (called vertebrates) have an inner ear. However, the shape and how it works can be different in different animals.

Example 235 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 235
Source Text
Among spinal subdural abscesses, methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus is the most common organism involved. Rarely parasites can cause abscesses and this is more common in the developing world.
Generated Simplification
Spinal subdural abscesses are infections that happen in the space around the spinal cord. - The most common cause of these infections is a type of bacteria called methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA). - In rare cases, parasites can also cause these infections. - Parasite-related abscesses are more likely to happen in parts of the world where healthcare resources are limited. If you have questions or concerns, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
Among spinal subdural abscesses, methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus is the most common organism involved.

Example 236 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 236
Source Text
Periungual warts are more common among patients who bite their nails or who have occupations where their hands are chronically wet such as dishwashers and bartenders. Genital warts manifest as discrete flat to broad-based smooth to velvety papules to rough and pedunculated excrescences on the perineal, perirectal, labial, and penile areas. Perirectal warts often itch.
Generated Simplification
Periungual warts are small growths that appear around the nails. - These warts are more common in people who bite their nails. - They also happen more often in people whose hands are often wet, like dishwashers or bartenders. Genital warts are growths that can appear in the private areas of the body. - They can look different. Some are flat and smooth, while others are rough or stick out. - These warts can show up around the genitals, near the anus, or on the skin of the penis or labia. - Warts near the anus (perirectal warts) can cause itching. If you notice any of these warts, talk to a doctor. They can help with treatment and advice.
Reference Simplification
Also called venereal warts or condylomata acuminata, genital warts occur on the penis, anus, vulva, vagina, and cervix. They can be flat, smooth, and velvety or irregular, bumpy growths often with the texture of a small cauliflower. Warts around the anus often itch.

The Intersex Society of North America stated that `` For decades, doctors have thought it necessary to treat intersex with a concealment-centered approach, one that features downplaying intersex as much as possible, even to the point of lying to patients about their conditions.
Generated Simplification
Doctors used to believe that intersex conditions should be handled by hiding them. - They often tried to make intersex seem less noticeable. - Sometimes, this included not telling patients the full truth about their condition. This approach was called a "concealment-centered approach."

Example 240 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 240
Source Text
Biopsy of the lung is usually not indicated unless the pulmonary hypertension is thought to be due to an underlying interstitial lung disease; further, lung biopsies are fraught with risks of bleeding due to the high intrapulmonary blood pressure.
Generated Simplification
A lung biopsy is not usually needed for pulmonary hypertension. Pulmonary hypertension means high blood pressure in the lungs. A biopsy is a test where a small piece of tissue is taken to look for problems. Doctors may only suggest a lung biopsy if they think the high blood pressure is caused by another lung problem, like interstitial lung disease. Interstitial lung disease is a condition that affects the tissue in the lungs. Lung biopsies can be risky. They can cause bleeding because the blood pressure in the lungs is already high. If you have questions about this, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
Biopsy of the lung is usually not indicated unless the pulmonary hypertension is thought to be due to an underlying interstitial lung disease.

0.364

Example 241 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 241
Source Text
Epidemiological studies can only go to prove that an agent could have caused, but not that it did cause, an effect in any particular case: In United States law, epidemiology alone can not prove that a causal association does not exist in general.
Generated Simplification
Scientists study how diseases spread and what causes them. This is called epidemiology. - These studies can show that something *might* cause a health problem. - However, they cannot prove that it *did* cause the problem in one specific person. In the United States, the law says that epidemiology alone cannot prove that something never causes a health problem.
Reference Simplification
Epidemiological studies can only go to prove that an agent could have caused disease in population, but not that it did cause, an effect in any particular case.

Example 242 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 242
Source Text
Signs occur along a continuum: no changes to varicose veins (rare) to edema to stasis dermatitis on the lower legs and at the ankles, with or without ulceration (see Table: Clinical Classification of Chronic Venous Insufficiency). Venous stasis dermatitis consists of reddish brown hyperpigmentation, induration, venous ectasia, lipodermatosclerosis (fibrosing subcutaneous panniculitis), and venous stasis ulcers.
Generated Simplification
Signs of this condition can vary. They can range from no visible changes in the veins (this is rare) to more noticeable problems. These problems may include: - Swelling in the legs (called edema). - Skin changes on the lower legs and ankles, known as stasis dermatitis. Stasis dermatitis can cause the following symptoms: - Reddish-brown discoloration of the skin. - Hardening or thickening of the skin. - Enlarged veins that are visible under the skin. - Tightening and thickening of the tissue under the skin (called lipodermatosclerosis). - Open sores on the skin, called venous stasis ulcers. If you notice any of these signs, talk to your doctor. They can help check your veins and skin and suggest the right treatment.
Reference Simplification
The discoloration is caused by red blood cells that escape from swollen (distended) veins into the skin. The discolored skin is vulnerable, and even a minor injury, such as that from scratching or a bump, can break it open, resulting in an ulcer.
BERTScore
Precision
0.114
Recall
0.209
F1
0.162

CL3 2 Different topics, such as general signs and specific symptoms, are separated into clear sections.

S1 2 Long sentences from the source are split into shorter, simpler sentences.
S3 2 Different ideas, such as general signs and specific symptoms, are clearly separated.

Example 243 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 243
Source Text
Most commonly found as small circular, double-stranded DNA molecules in bacteria, plasmids are sometimes present in archaea and eukaryotic organisms.
Generated Simplification
Plasmids are small pieces of DNA. - They are shaped like circles. - They have two strands of DNA. Plasmids are usually found in bacteria. Sometimes, they can also be found in other organisms like archaea and eukaryotes (types of living things).
Reference Simplification
Plasmids usually occur naturally in bacteria, but are sometimes found in eukaryotic organisms (e.g., the 2-micrometre-ring in Saccharomyces cerevisiae).

Example 245 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 245
Source Text
A common finding during physical examination is a symmetry or a dominant mass âa mass distinctly different from the surrounding breast tissue. Diffuse fibrotic changes in a quadrant of the breast, usually the upper outer quadrant, are more characteristic of benign disorders; a slightly firmer thickening in one breast but not the other may be a sign of cancer.
Generated Simplification
During a breast exam, doctors may notice certain changes. These changes can include: - A lump or mass that feels different from the rest of the breast tissue. - Changes in one part of the breast that feel firmer or thicker than usual. Here are some important points to know: - If the changes are spread out and feel like thickened tissue, especially in the upper outer part of the breast, this is often not cancer. It may be a benign (non-cancerous) condition. - If one breast has a firm or thick area that is not present in the other breast, this could be a sign of cancer. If you notice any changes in your breasts, talk to your doctor. They can check to see if further tests are needed.
Reference Simplification
Such a lump may be cancer if it is a firm, distinctive thickening that appears in one breast but not the other. Usually, scattered lumpy changes in the breast, especially the upper outer region, are not cancerous and indicate fibrocystic changes.

Precision
0.207
Recall
0.235

0.222

Example 246 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 246
Source Text
More advanced breast cancers are characterized by one or more of the following: Fixation of the mass to the chest wall or to overlying skin; Satellite nodules or ulcers in the skin; Exaggeration of the usual skin markings resulting from skin edema caused by invasion of dermal lymphatic vessels (so-called peau d â orange).
Generated Simplification
Breast cancer can become more advanced over time. This means it may show certain signs that need attention. These signs include: - A lump in the breast that is stuck to the chest wall or the skin above it. - Small lumps or sores on the skin near the main lump. - Changes in the skin of the breast, such as swelling that makes the skin look like an orange peel. This happens because the cancer affects tiny vessels in the skin. If you notice any of these changes, talk to your doctor. They can check what is happening and help you understand your options.
Reference Simplification
In more advanced stages, the lump usually adheres to the chest wall or the skin over it. If a breast contains cancer that adheres to the chest wall or skin, this maneuver may make the skin pucker or dimple or make one breast appear different from the other. In very advanced cancer, swollen bumps or festering sores may develop on the skin. Sometimes the skin over the lump is dimpled and leathery and looks like the skin of an orange (peau d â orange) except in color.

Example 247 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 247
Source Text
Two leading hypotheses suggest it may have jumped species from the disease scrapie in sheep, or that it evolved from a spontaneous form of `` mad cow disease '' that has been seen occasionally in cattle for many centuries.
Generated Simplification
Scientists have two main ideas about where this disease might have come from: 1. It might have spread from a disease in sheep called scrapie. 2. It might have started on its own in cows. This is a rare condition sometimes called "mad cow disease," and it has been seen in cattle for hundreds of years.
Reference Simplification
Mad cow disease has damaged the cattle industry.

Source Text
Myoclonus that occurs when patients are suddenly startled (startle myoclonus) may be an early symptom of Creutzfeldt-Jacob disease.
Generated Simplification
Sometimes, people may experience sudden jerking movements when they are startled. This is called startle myoclonus. Startle myoclonus can sometimes be an early sign of a condition called Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease. If you or someone you know has sudden jerking movements like this, it is important to talk to a doctor. They can check for possible causes and provide advice.
Reference Simplification
In Creutzfeldt-Jacob disease (a rare degenerative brain disorder), myoclonus becomes more obvious when people are suddenly startled.

Example 253 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 253
Source Text
Because patients lack the motor responses (eg, withdrawal from painful stimuli) usually used to measure responsiveness, they may be mistakenly thought to be unconscious. Thus, all patients who can not move should have their comprehension tested by requesting eye blinking or vertical eye movements.
Generated Simplification
Sometimes, patients cannot move their bodies to respond to pain or other tests. This can make it seem like they are unconscious, even if they are not. For patients who cannot move, doctors should check if they can understand what is being said. This can be done by asking the patient to: - Blink their eyes. - Move their eyes up and down. These simple actions can show if the patient is aware and able to understand.
Reference Simplification
Because locked-in syndrome can be mistaken for stupor or coma, doctors test people who do not move and appear unresponsive by asking them to open and close their eyes.

Example 254 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 254
Source Text
Bedside neurologic testing Neuropsychologic testing
Generated Simplification
### Bedside Neurologic Testing This is a simple check that doctors do to see how well your brain, nerves, and muscles are working. It is done while you are in the hospital or clinic. The doctor may ask you to do things like move your arms or legs, follow a light with your eyes, or answer some questions. These tests help the doctor find out if there are any problems with your brain or nerves. ### Neuropsychologic Testing This is a more detailed test to check how your brain works. It looks at things like memory, attention, problem-solving, and how you understand and use language. A trained specialist will guide you through different tasks, like solving puzzles or remembering words. These tests can help find problems caused by brain injuries, illnesses, or other conditions. If you need this test, your doctor will explain it to you and answer any questions.
Reference Simplification
A doctor 's evaluation Standardized tests of brain function
BERTScore
Precision
-0.171
Recall
0.386
F1
0.094

Example 257 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 257
Source Text
Cardiac complications include pericarditis (most commonly) and myocarditis.
Generated Simplification
Heart problems can happen. - The most common problem is pericarditis. This means swelling or irritation of the thin layer around the heart. - Another problem is myocarditis. This means swelling in the heart muscle. If you have questions or concerns, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
People with lupus may have chest pain due to inflammation of the sac around the heart pericarditis). More serious but rare effects on the heart are inflammation of the walls of the coronary arteries (coronary artery vasculitis), which can lead to angina, and inflammation of the heart muscle (myocarditis), which can lead to heart failure.

Example 265 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 265
Source Text
In developing countries, most cases are probably caused by tuberculosis, particularly in patients with impaired immune function due to undernutrition or human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection.
Generated Simplification
In some countries, many people get sick because of tuberculosis (TB). This happens more often in people whose immune system is weak. The immune system helps protect the body from illness. A weak immune system can happen if: - A person does not get enough food (this is called undernutrition). - A person has an infection called HIV (human immunodeficiency virus). If you have questions about TB, undernutrition, or HIV, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
In developing nations, severe or repeated infections that cause bronchiectasis are more often caused by tuberculosis.

Example 271 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 271

Renovascular hypertension may develop if the abdominal aorta or renal arteries are narrowed.

High blood pressure, called hypertension, can happen if certain blood vessels in your body become too narrow. - These blood vessels include the abdominal aorta, which is a large artery in your belly. - It can also happen if the renal arteries, which bring blood to your kidneys, become narrow. Reference Simplification

Example 272 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 272
Source Text
Unlike other dementias, multiple- infarct dementia tends to progress in discrete steps; each episode is accompanied by intellectual decline, sometimes followed by modest recovery.
Generated Simplification
Multiple-infarct dementia is a type of dementia. It does not get worse in a steady way like some other types. Instead, it gets worse in steps. Each step happens after an episode, such as a stroke or a similar event. When this happens, a person’s thinking and memory may get worse. Sometimes, there may be a small improvement after the episode, but it is usually not complete.
Reference Simplification
Unlike dementia caused by Alzheimer disease, vascular dementia may progress in steps. Symptoms may worsen suddenly, then plateau or lessen somewhat. They then become worse months or years later when another stroke occurs.

Example 273 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 273
Source Text
Intractable apneic spells require ventilator support.
Generated Simplification
Some breathing problems are very serious and do not stop on their own. These are called intractable apneic spells. When this happens, a machine called a ventilator may be needed. A ventilator helps a person breathe by moving air in and out of their lungs. If you or someone you care for has this problem, talk to a doctor right away.
Reference Simplification
Newborns who have apnea spells that are difficult to treat may need a ventilator (a machine that helps air get in and out of the lungs) to help them breathe.

Example 274 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 274
Source Text
Diagnosis of DiGeorge syndrome is based on clinical findings. An absolute lymphocyte count is done, followed by B- and T- cell counts and evaluation of lymphocyte subsets if leukopenia is detected; blood tests to evaluate T- cell and parathyroid function are done. Ig levels and vaccine titers are measured. If complete DiGeorge syndrome is suspected, the T- cell receptor excision circle (TREC) test should also be done. A lateral chest x-ray may help evaluate thymic shadow. Fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH) testing can detect the chromosomal deletion in the 22q11 region; standard chromosomal tests to check for other abnormalities may also be done.
Generated Simplification
Doctors diagnose DiGeorge syndrome by looking at symptoms and doing specific tests. Here’s how they check for it: ### Step 1: Blood Tests - A blood test checks the number of lymphocytes (a type of white blood cell). - If the lymphocyte count is low, doctors will check B-cells and T-cells (types of immune cells) and look at different types of lymphocytes. - They also test how well T-cells and the parathyroid gland are working. - Levels of immunoglobulins (Ig), which are proteins that help fight infections, are measured. - Vaccine titers are checked to see how well the body responds to vaccines. ### Step 2: Special Tests for Severe Cases - If doctors think the condition is severe (called complete DiGeorge syndrome), they may do a TREC test. This test looks for a specific marker in T-cells. ### Step 3: Imaging - A chest x-ray may be done to look for the thymus gland. The thymus helps the immune system, and its shadow can sometimes be seen on the x-ray. ### Step 4: Genetic Testing - A test called FISH (fluorescent in situ hybridization) can find a missing piece of chromosome 22 in the 22q11 region. This is a common cause of DiGeorge syndrome. - Other genetic tests may be done to check for additional problems with chromosomes. If you have questions about these tests or think they might apply to you or your child, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
Sometimes imaging tests (such as chest x-rays and echocardiography). Doctors suspect DiGeorge syndrome based on symptoms. Blood tests are done for the following reasons: To determine the total number of blood cells and the number of T and B cells; To evaluate how well T cells and the parathyroid gland are functioning; To determine how well the body produces immunoglobulins in response to vaccines. A chest x-ray may be taken to check the size of the thymus gland.

Precision
0.057
Recall
0.284

0.169

L3 2 Abbreviations like FISH and TREC are clarified in the text.
A3 2 Implied information, such as the purpose of each test, is made explicit to avoid misunderstanding.
Overall checklist comment
The simplification is well-structured, uses clear language, and effectively reduces cognitive load for readers with cognitive disabilities.

Example 275 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 275
Source Text
It had a cranial capacity of 400-500 cc. In 2006, Lee Berger announced the Taung Child probably was killed by an eagle or other large predatory bird since the similarity of damage to the skull and eye sockets of the Taung Child is of that seen in modern primates that are known to have been killed by eagles.
Generated Simplification
The Taung Child had a brain size of about 400 to 500 cubic centimeters (cc). In 2006, a researcher named Lee Berger shared an important finding. He said the Taung Child was likely killed by an eagle or a big bird that hunts animals. This idea came from studying the damage to the skull and eye sockets of the Taung Child. The injuries looked similar to those found in modern monkeys that were attacked and killed by eagles.
Reference Simplification
This conclusion was reached by noting similarities in the damage to the skull and eye sockets of the Taung Child to the skulls of primates known to have been killed by modern eagles. The left hand side of the endocast of the Taung child is covered in beautiful calcium carbonate crystals.

Example 276 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 276
Source Text
Renal involvement can develop at any time and may be the only manifestation of SLE. It may be benign and asymptomatic or progressive and fatal.
Generated Simplification
Kidney problems can happen at any time in people with SLE (systemic lupus erythematosus). - SLE is a condition where the immune system attacks the body. - Kidney problems might be the only sign of SLE in some people. These kidney issues can be mild and cause no symptoms. However, they can also get worse over time and become life-threatening.
Reference Simplification
Kidney involvement may be minor and without symptoms or may be progressive and fatal. The kidneys can be affected at any time and may be the only organ affected by lupus.

Example 278 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 278
Source Text
Vasoconstriction can change a lesion that does not limit blood flow into a severe or complete stenosis. Symptoms of unstable angina or myocardial infarction, ischemic stroke, or rest pain in the limbs may develop when unstable plaques rupture and acutely occlude a major artery, with superimposition of thrombosis or embolism.
Generated Simplification
When blood vessels tighten (this is called vasoconstriction), it can make a mild blockage in the blood flow worse. In some cases, it can cause a severe or complete blockage. Here are some problems that can happen if a major artery gets blocked suddenly: - Chest pain or discomfort that may signal unstable angina (a type of heart problem). - A heart attack (also called a myocardial infarction). - A stroke caused by reduced blood flow to the brain (called an ischemic stroke). - Pain in the arms or legs, even when resting (this is called rest pain). These problems can happen if a weak spot in the artery (called an unstable plaque) breaks open. This can lead to a blood clot (thrombosis) or a piece of the clot traveling to another part of the body (embolism). If you have symptoms like these, talk to a doctor right away.
Reference Simplification
The first symptom of a narrowed artery may be pain or cramps at times when blood flow can not keep up with the tissues â need for oxygen.

Precision
0.021
Recall
0.335
F1
0.175

Example 279 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 279
Source Text
According to the fifth Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM), DID includes `` the presence of two or more distinct identities or personality states '' that alternate control of the individual 's behavior, accompanied by the inability to recall personal information beyond what is expected through normal forgetfulness.
Generated Simplification
Dissociative Identity Disorder (DID) is a mental health condition. - A person with DID has two or more different identities or personality states. - These identities take turns controlling the person’s behavior. - People with DID often cannot remember personal information. This forgetfulness is more severe than normal memory lapses. If you think you or someone you know might have DID, talk to a healthcare professional.
Reference Simplification
According to the DSM-IV, the symptoms of DID are `` the presence of two or more distinct identities or personality states '' (which are often called `` alters ''), as well as forgetting things that people normally do n't forget.

Example 298 | Profile: cognitive_disabilities | Language: English | ID/Index: 298
Source Text
Arterial blood carries oxygen from inhaled air to all of the cells of the body, and venous blood carries carbon dioxide, a waste product of metabolism by cells, to the lungs to be exhaled.
Generated Simplification
Your blood has two main jobs: - **Arterial blood** (blood in your arteries) carries oxygen from the air you breathe to all parts of your body. - **Venous blood** (blood in your veins) carries carbon dioxide, which is a waste your body makes, back to your lungs. Your lungs then get rid of the carbon dioxide when you breathe out.
Reference Simplification
Red blood cells carry oxygen and carbon dioxide around our body.
